[Conjure](#Conjure) builds run in an [egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) environment with digest-pinned toolchains, producing full [SLSA](#Provenance) attestation and [SBOMs](#SBOM). This is the highest-trust build mode. **[Bind](#Bind)** — Mirror an upstream image pinned by digest. [Binding](#Bind) captures an external image at a specific digest into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. Trust is established through digest-pin verification rather than build [provenance](#Provenance). **[Graft](#Graft)** — Push a locally-built image to the [Depot](#Depot) registry. [Grafting](#Graft) uploads a local image to GAR via docker push — no Cloud Build for the image itself, though [About](#About) and [Vouch](#Vouch) metadata still run in Cloud Build. This is the lowest-trust mode (GRAFTED verdict). **[Kludge](#Kludge)** — Build a [Vessel](#Vessel) image locally for fast iteration, without [Depot](#Depot) registry push. [Kludging](#Kludge) produces a local Docker image for development and testing without involving Cloud Build or the [Depot](#Depot). The resulting image can be used to [Charge](#Charge) a [Crucible](#Crucible) directly. **[Pouch](#Pouch)** — Build context packaged as a FROM SCRATCH OCI image and pushed to the [Depot's](#Depot) registry before a Cloud Build job runs. The [Director](#Director) controls what enters the [Pouch](#Pouch) — Dockerfile, context files, build scripts — and the cloud receives only what the [Pouch](#Pouch) contains. This is the security boundary between workstation and build infrastructure. ### Verification **[Tally](#Tally)** — Inventory [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) in the [Depot](#Depot) registry by health status. [Tallying](#Tally) shows which builds succeeded, which are pending, and which failed. The [Director](#Director) [Tallies](#Tally) before [Vouching](#Vouch) to confirm build completion. **[Rekon](#Rekon)** — Raw listing of image tags in the [Depot](#Depot) registry for a [Vessel](#Vessel) package. [Rekon](#Rekon) is a [Director](#Director)-only diagnostic that shows exactly what exists in the registry without health interpretation. Where [Tally](#Tally) groups [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) by status, [Rekon](#Rekon) shows the unprocessed tag inventory. **[Vouch](#Vouch)** — Cryptographic attestation proving a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) was built by trusted infrastructure. The [Vouch](#Vouch) verdict is mode-aware: [Conjure](#Conjure) builds receive full [SLSA provenance](#Provenance) verification, [Bind](#Bind) builds receive digest-pin verification, and [Graft](#Graft) builds receive a GRAFTED verdict with no [provenance](#Provenance) chain. The [Director](#Director) [Vouches](#Vouch) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) after [Tallying](#Tally) their build status.

content.content: 490 - **Build-over-build drift detection** — compare [SBOMs](#SBOM) across [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) to see what changed between builds 491 492 The [Plumb](#Plumb) command surfaces [SBOM](#SBOM) contents: the full view shows package inventories; the compact view summarizes key components. 493 494 ## Appendix: Build Isolation 495 496 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) supports two build egress profiles — [Tethered](#Tethered) and [Airgap](#Airgap) — that determine whether a Cloud Build job can reach the public internet. 497 The distinction is not primarily about availability; it is a security boundary that controls what can enter and exit the build environment. 498 499 **What [Airgap](#Airgap) protects: exfiltration and supply chain injection.** 500 If a compromised dependency, build plugin, or Dockerfile instruction executes during your build, an [Airgapped](#Airgap) build cannot phone home — it cannot transmit source code, secrets, or intermediate artifacts to an external endpoint, and it cannot silently fetch malicious payloads. 501 This is defense-in-depth for proprietary code: even if a build step is compromised, the network is not available as an exfiltration channel. 502 503 **The curated gate principle.** 504 [Airgap](#Airgap) does not mean "nothing external." It means all external content enters through a single auditable gate — the [Enshrine](#Enshrine) ceremony — rather than ad-hoc network fetches during build. 505 The attack surface collapses from "any URL a Dockerfile mentions" to "the specific digests the [Director](#Director) [Enshrined](#Enshrine)." 506 Builder tool images enter through a parallel gate: the [Reliquary](#Reliquary), inscribed once and pinned by digest for all subsequent builds. 507 508 **What [Airgap](#Airgap) does not protect: the base image contents.** 509 Base images like `debian-slim` were themselves built with full internet access — `apt-get install` already ran inside them. 510 The [Airgap](#Airgap) protects *your* build steps on top of those bases, not the base image contents themselves. 511 Base images are vetted separately: digest-pinned at [Enshrine](#Enshrine) time, inspectable via [SBOM](#SBOM), and stored as project-owned copies in the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 512 A [Tethered](#Tethered) build of the base image followed by an [Airgapped](#Airgap) build of your application is the expected pattern — the base image is a known input, your proprietary layers are the protected output. 513 514 **Regulatory alignment.** 515 No framework mandates build-time network blocking by name, but egress-locked builds are the simplest way to evidence several common controls: FedRAMP CM-7 (least functionality) and SC-7 (boundary protection), SOC 2 CC6.1 (logical access) and CC8.1 (change management), and SLSA Level 3's hermetic build requirement. 516 517 ## Appendix: Eventual Consistency and the Missing Completion Contract 518 519 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is built on cloud APIs, and cloud APIs are *eventually consistent*: when you mutate state — grant a role, delete a [service account](#Governor), link billing — the change does not take effect everywhere at once. 520 It propagates across replicas over seconds, occasionally minutes. 521 For systems that are read on essentially every API call across the globe, choosing fast, always-available reads over instantly-consistent ones is a defensible engineering tradeoff, and we grant it without complaint. 522 523 The defensible part is the consistency *model*. 524 The indefensible part is what the term quietly omits: a **completion contract**. 525 When a mutating call returns, it tells you nothing about whether the work is finished — there is no terminal-state signal you can poll to learn that the change has settled. 526 This is not a law of physics. 527 The pattern for providing it is well understood and widely shipped: Google's own API design guidelines define long-running operations with a `done` flag and a terminal state, and Azure's Resource Manager specifies an async-operation contract end to end. 528 The giants deliver it excellently in places — and then withhold it on exactly the operations that race: IAM propagation, billing linkage, identity lifecycle. 529 The capability exists; it is selectively absent where it would matter most. 530 531 Without a completion contract, every consumer independently reinvents the same retry-poll-tolerate scaffolding to compensate. 532 The honest version of that scaffolding polls for an *observable terminal state*; the dishonest version, which the gap quietly encourages, is a blind `sleep N` — a guessed magic number standing in for a signal that should have existed. 533 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) holds the honest line where it can: it polls for the real state, requires *consecutive* confirming reads before believing a transition (debouncing the flap rather than trusting the first answer), and bounds every wait with a timeout so a never-settling operation fails loudly instead of hanging. 534 535 The sharpest instance is service-account deletion. 536 The delete returns an empty success with no operation handle, and the account is not actually removed — it is *soft-deleted*, recoverable for thirty days. 537 So even the simplest question, "is it gone; can I reuse the name?", has no clean answer: a read of the just-deleted account flaps between "present" and "absent" across replicas while the tombstone propagates, and no API will tell you when the name is safe to reuse. 538 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) [Divest](#Divest) path copes by treating the account as durably gone only after several consecutive "not found" reads — see the ["already exists" after a delete](#StaleDeleteRead) diagnostic for the symptom this produces. 539 540 Plainly: this is not a hard distributed-systems problem. 541 It is a completion contract the vendors chose not to provide, dressed in distributed-systems vocabulary. 542 "Eventual consistency" accurately describes the read path; here it is also a polite way of saying *we will not tell you when we are finished*. 543 We depend on Google Cloud and expect to keep depending on it — and building atop eventually-consistent APIs with no completion contract is still crappy engineering on the vendor's part, on exactly the surfaces where getting it right would cost them nothing in their consistency model. 544 545 ## Appendix: Registry Churn and Disappearing Upstream Images 546 547 The [Foundry](#Foundry) builds on images pulled from upstream registries. Most are durable — an image pinned by digest stays fetchable, and a published checksum lets you re-verify the bytes later. Some are not. The sharpest case we have hit is Quay's `quay.io/podman/machine-os` family, the disk images a `podman machine` boots: Quay churns them rapidly — new images every few hours, retention measured in days — so a reference that resolved this morning can be gone by tomorrow. For Quay's own use that is fine, and we grant it; for anyone who needs a *reproducible* build it is a trap. The indefensible part is not the churn but what comes with it: no durable reference. Quay retains no digest you can pin and publishes no checksum to verify against later, so a pinned build does not fail loudly when its base ages out — it breaks asynchronously, downstream, on someone else's clock. That is not hypothetical; it is the failure that motivated this work. 548 549 The response is the **[Lode](#Lode)**: a project-owned copy of an upstream artifact — a base image, a build tool, an OS substrate, a VM disk image — captured once into the [Depot's](#Depot) own registry and held with a provenance record, so the bytes a build depends on cannot be pulled out from under it. Recipe Bottle grades its confidence honestly rather than uniformly: where the upstream is durable a Lode is *verified-against-published*, its bytes still re-checkable against the source; where it is not — as with the podman machine-os images — the Lode carries the weaker but honest grade *recorded-at-acquisition*, attesting the exact digest captured and claiming nothing beyond it, because the upstream permits nothing beyond it. A registry that ships images with no durable reference has decided reproducibility is not its concern; holding our own copy, and grading our confidence in it honestly, is the only sound way to build on an upstream that will not hold still. 550 551 ## Appendix: Roadmap 552 553 The following features are not yet implemented but are under consideration: 554 555 - **[Crucible Conduit for Cloud Services](#CrucibleConduit)** - Encrypted tunnel from the [Sentry](#Sentry) to a VPC hosting PrivateLink endpoints, enabling [Bottles](#Bottle) to reach cloud AI services (AWS Bedrock, Vertex AI, Azure OpenAI) without exposing floating cloud IP ranges in the CIDR allowlist. 556 WireGuard terminated at the [Sentry](#Sentry) replaces per-service IP tracking with a single stable VPC CIDR. 557 Near-term, allowlist-only [Nameplates](#Nameplate) targeting specific service CIDRs and domains work today with existing [Sentry](#Sentry) machinery. 558 The tunnel adds defense-in-depth for PrivateLink-capable services; SaaS endpoints without PrivateLink (GitHub.com, GitLab.com) remain served by CIDR/domain allowlisting. 559 560 - **[Bottle Credential Custody](#BottleCredentialCustody)** - Move the service secrets a [Bottle](#Bottle) workload uses (cloud API keys, IAM keys, SSH keys) off the operator's workstation and into the [Bottle](#Bottle) itself, injected at [Charge](#Charge) time via [Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Regime](#Regime) configuration — never baked into the image. 561 The workstation [Charges](#Charge) the [Crucible](#Crucible) but never holds the secret; its only credential becomes permission to charge, which blocks credential theft from a compromised workstation. 562 Orthogonal to but paired with the [Crucible Conduit](#CrucibleConduit): the conduit gives the [Bottle](#Bottle) network reach to a cloud service while the [Sentry](#Sentry) holds the tunnel key, and this holds the service secret inside the [Bottle](#Bottle). 563 564 - **[Operator Federation](#OperatorFederation)** - The path to corporate-acceptable identity — admit operators through an external identity provider (OIDC/SAML) instead of long-lived service-account keys on disk, with identity proved by a fresh sign-in and capabilities granted to a federated principal, no secret at rest. 565 This tier **requires a GCP organization**, which is the dividing line from today's keyfile model (which needs none): a qualifying organization is free via Google Cloud Identity once you verify ownership of a **DNS domain**, so the real prerequisite is controlling a domain, not paying Google. 566 Revocation becomes central at the identity provider rather than a manual, per-key [Divest](#Divest). 567 Critical sequences, rendered from committed PlantUML source through the [pluml](#pluml) [Crucible](#Crucible) — each diagram tracks your light/dark color scheme (click any to expand): 568 569

67:**Revised enshrine — universal capture verb under Lode recipe noun.**
68:Today's narrow `enshrine` mirrors only upstream OCI base images.
69:Three additional capture needs share the same gesture (pull from upstream, SLSA-wrap, store in GAR): build-time tool images (today's `mirror`/`reliquary` machinery), the WSL substrate distro that hosts the Windows workload, and podman VM disk images.
70:The revision generalizes `enshrine` into a universal verb operating on a single recipe noun — **Lode**, parallel to {rbtga_vessel} on the built side — with a kind enum that gates pipeline behavior: `base`, `tool`, `reliquary`, `wsl`, `podvm-wsl`, `podvm-native`.
71:Today's `reliquary` demotes from top-level noun to a kind enum slot — the date-cohort semantic survives, the cult-vocabulary weight drops.
73:absorbed into enshrine on the `tool` and `reliquary` kinds.
74:Lode .env carries author intent only — no resolved FQIN field — mirroring RBRV's ORIGIN/ANCHOR pattern (kindle code computes upstream coordinates from kind primitives at module-startup).
77:_Trigger_: when Windows host workload requires a project-controlled WSL distro, when podman VM supply chain needs project-owned bytes in GAR, or when the `mirror`/`reliquary` machinery accumulates enough friction to motivate consolidation.
78:_Reference_: RBSLE-lode_ensconce.adoc (the bole-kind capture spec);
261:Operation-level routing: inscribe/enshrine use tether, about/vouch always airgap, conjure/bind per vessel `RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`.
279:**Lode vouch signing — confirm GAR referrers, decide cosign-vs-referrers.**
280:The Lode provenance envelope rides today as a reserved `:rbi_vouch` tag (one per Lode), schema-versioned with `signature: null` — a clean upgrade seam reserved without spending now.
281:When signing becomes warranted, confirm whether GAR's OCI 1.1 referrers API is mature enough to attach attestation as a subject-linked artifact, and decide between that and cosign for the Lode vouch.
284:_Reference_: RBS0-SpecTop.adoc (Lode provenance envelope, `rbld-vouch-2`).

**SA preflight universalization.**
Currently only
{rbtoe_iam_grant_sa}
performs a pre-flight existence check on the target
{giam_service_account}.
Applying it to all grant types when the member is a service account requires conditional logic and changes behavior for non-SA members.
_Trigger_: if grant failures due to non-existent members become a diagnostic problem.
_Reference_:
<<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>
(Convergence Analysis section).

**Podman as first-class deployment target.**
Recipe Bottle currently deploys to Podman on workstations via the bottle lifecycle (RBSBS, RBSBR, RBSBL).
Podman support is functional but not hardened — the sentry container, network isolation, and eBPF enforcement assume Linux Podman with rootful access.
Expanding to rootless Podman, Podman on macOS (via podman-machine), or alternative container runtimes would require revisiting the security model and network architecture.
_Trigger_: user demand for rootless deployment or macOS-native workflows beyond the current Podman VM approach.
_Reference_: RBSPV-PodmanVmSupplyChain.adoc (current VM supply chain);
RBSBS, RBSBR, RBSBL (bottle lifecycle specs).

**`podman-compose` lacks `--wait` for `compose up`.**
Docker Compose `--wait` (v2.1.1+) blocks until all container health checks pass, which `rbob_charge` relies on to guarantee crucible readiness before returning.
`podman-compose` does not implement `--wait` — the command returns immediately after scheduling containers, creating a race between charge completion and the active-check assertion in theurge.
Without `--wait`, Podman integration will need an alternative readiness gate (polling loop, explicit health-check query, or `podman wait --condition=healthy`).
_Trigger_: Podman first-class deployment (above).
_Reference_: containers/podman-compose#710 (June 2023, open);
containers/podman-compose#1329 (October 2025, open).

**Revised enshrine — universal capture verb under Lode recipe noun.**
Today's narrow `enshrine` mirrors only upstream OCI base images.
Three additional capture needs share the same gesture (pull from upstream, SLSA-wrap, store in GAR): build-time tool images (today's `mirror`/`reliquary` machinery), the WSL substrate distro that hosts the Windows workload, and podman VM disk images.
The revision generalizes `enshrine` into a universal verb operating on a single recipe noun — **Lode**, parallel to {rbtga_vessel} on the built side — with a kind enum that gates pipeline behavior: `base`, `tool`, `reliquary`, `wsl`, `podvm-wsl`, `podvm-native`.
Today's `reliquary` demotes from top-level noun to a kind enum slot — the date-cohort semantic survives, the cult-vocabulary weight drops.
Today's `mirror` verb retires entirely;
absorbed into enshrine on the `tool` and `reliquary` kinds.
Lode .env carries author intent only — no resolved FQIN field — mirroring RBRV's ORIGIN/ANCHOR pattern (kindle code computes upstream coordinates from kind primitives at module-startup).
The `wsl` kind delivers a project-controlled WSL rootfs tar that replaces the Microsoft-Store-appx-mutable distribution today's caparison-w depends on;
the `podvm-*` kinds collapse `rbv_PodmanVM.sh`'s existing GHCR mirror into GAR under the unified verb.
_Trigger_: when Windows host workload requires a project-controlled WSL distro, when podman VM supply chain needs project-owned bytes in GAR, or when the `mirror`/`reliquary` machinery accumulates enough friction to motivate consolidation.
_Reference_: RBSLE-lode_ensconce.adoc (the bole-kind capture spec);
RBSRV-RegimeVessel.adoc (ORIGIN/ANCHOR precedent, kind-enum-gated variable groups pattern);
`Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/FUTURE/rbv_PodmanVM.sh` (existing podman-vm fan-out machinery).

**Hairpin peer access between crucibles.**
Bottled containers cannot currently reach each other's published entry ports.
Each bottle is isolated behind its own sentry on a separate enclave network.
The traffic path is feasible: Bottle A egresses through Sentry A's transit bridge, the host kernel routes to Sentry B's transit bridge, and Sentry B's existing entry path (DNAT + RBM-FORWARD) accepts the connection unchanged.
The security model is preserved — the sentry remains the sole enforcement point, with peer access as a new category of allowed destination alongside entry (inbound) and uplink (outbound internet).
Nameplate model: two new variables (`RBRN_PEER_ACCESS_MODE` disabled|allowlist, gated `RBRN_PEER_ALLOWED_MONIKERS` as comma-separated moniker list).
No global mode — peer access must always be explicit.
Implementation touches three areas: nameplate kindle/validate/render, a peer-access iptables step in security_config, and host-side peer IP discovery via container inspection.
Audit gains a cross-instance check: each named peer must exist and have entry enabled.
_Trigger_: when a use case requires bottle-to-bottle communication (e.g., microservice composition, service mesh patterns).

**Chantry — Bedrock-hosted inference endpoint.**
Architecturally distinct from the embassy/bullion pattern.
A chantry is a Bedrock-registered custom model (or a native Bedrock foundation model) invoked by a
{at_bottle_container}
over the crucible conduit architecture — not an artifact Recipe Bottle delivers, but a foreign-sovereign service a bottle consumes.
Weights may originate as a bullion (entrusted to an embassy, then consumed by Bedrock Custom Model Import on the AWS side), but the chantry itself is the registered-and-invocable endpoint, not the weight storage.
Pairs with the "Cloud service conduit nameplate" entry for the invocation path.
_Trigger_: first concrete Bedrock inference requirement;
near-term per the Crucible Conduit Architecture section above.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
`Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md`;
Crucible Conduit Architecture section above.
NOTE: the native-consume half of this entry is renamed and currently shaped as the **Quire** in `Memos/memo-20260609-bedrock-quire-shaping.md` (vocabulary, privacy boundaries, consume-side governance: Precentor/Chorister/Prebend/Cloister, deployment ladder); the custom-model-import half stays with the embassy/bullion delivery path above and remains unshaped.

**Egress lockdown — IMPLEMENTED.**
Dual-pool architecture: tether pool (public egress) and airgap pool (`NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS`).
Operation-level routing: inscribe/enshrine use tether, about/vouch always airgap, conjure/bind per vessel `RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`.
Reliquaries (tool images) and anchors (base images) provide universal GAR-sourced dependencies.
_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Implemented: Egress Lockdown via Dual Pools).

**VPC Service Controls perimeter.**
Wrap depot project in VPC-SC perimeter restricting API access.
Requires egress lockdown (now implemented) as prerequisite, org-level admin for perimeter creation, and verification of every GCP API call in the pipeline.
_Trigger_: organizational security policy mandating VPC-SC, or data exfiltration risk assessment.
_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Potential Hardening: VPC Service Controls).
Cross-reference correction (`Memos/memo-20260609-bedrock-quire-shaping.md`): the conduit/Outpost VPC is an *AWS* network and VPC-SC is a *GCP* service perimeter around the depot project — different clouds; neither can serve the other. The two items are independent.

**Cosign image signing.**
Cryptographic signatures on images, verifiable by downstream consumers.
KMS-backed keyed signing preferred for airgap-pool compatibility.
Currently redundant given CB-native SLSA v1.0 Build Level 3 attestation.
_Trigger_: external image distribution, trust verification needed outside GCP, or builder strategy expanding beyond Google Cloud Build.
_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Cosign).

**Lode vouch signing — confirm GAR referrers, decide cosign-vs-referrers.**
The Lode provenance envelope rides today as a reserved `:rbi_vouch` tag (one per Lode), schema-versioned with `signature: null` — a clean upgrade seam reserved without spending now.
When signing becomes warranted, confirm whether GAR's OCI 1.1 referrers API is mature enough to attach attestation as a subject-linked artifact, and decide between that and cosign for the Lode vouch.
Verifying GAR referrers maturity is a vendor (Palisade) question with no consumer until distribution leaves the project, so it is deliberately not a pace.
_Trigger_: same as Cosign — external image distribution or builder strategy beyond Google Cloud Build.
_Reference_: RBS0-SpecTop.adoc (Lode provenance envelope, `rbld-vouch-2`).

**Binary Authorization.**
Deploy-time policy enforcement (only attested images can be deployed).
Technically achievable now with CB-native SLSA Level 3.
Not beneficial until deployment targets include GKE or Cloud Run.

lines: ### Supply Chain
-**[Enshrine](#Enshrine)** — Mirror upstream base images into your [Depot's](#Depot) registry.

+[Capturing](#Capture) ensures the build pipeline has a fixed, self-contained supply chain — builds draw from project-owned copies rather than depending on third-party registry availability at build time.

+Each artifact kind — base image, builder toolset, OS substrate, VM disk image — has its own capture operation, but every capture produces the same thing: a [Lode](#Lode) with a provenance record, named by its [Touchmark](#Touchmark).

+Builder tool images are [Captured](#Capture) together as one co-versioned [Lode](#Lode); Cloud Build jobs use its members as step containers, ensuring builds run with known, project-owned toolchains rather than pulling tools from upstream at build time.

-Cloud Build jobs use [Reliquary](#Reliquary) images as step containers, ensuring builds run with known, project-owned toolchains rather than pulling tools from upstream at build time.
-The [Director](#Director) inscribes a [Reliquary](#Reliquary) before any [Ordain](#Ordain) or [Enshrine](#Enshrine) operation can run.

input.old_string:
**Revised enshrine — universal capture verb under Lode recipe noun.**
Today's narrow `enshrine` mirrors only upstream OCI base images.
Three additional capture needs share the same gesture (pull from upstream, SLSA-wrap, store in GAR): build-time tool images (today's `mirror`/`reliquary` machinery), the WSL substrate distro that hosts the Windows workload, and podman VM disk images.
The revision generalizes `enshrine` into a universal verb operating on a single recipe noun — **Lode**, parallel to {rbtga_vessel} on the built side — with a kind enum that gates pipeline behavior: `base`, `tool`, `reliquary`, `wsl`, `podvm-wsl`, `podvm-native`.
Today's `reliquary` demotes from top-level noun to a kind enum slot — the date-cohort semantic survives, the cult-vocabulary weight drops.
Today's `mirror` verb retires entirely;
absorbed into enshrine on the `tool` and `reliquary` kinds.
Lode .env carries author intent only — no resolved FQIN field — mirroring RBRV's ORIGIN/ANCHOR pattern (kindle code computes upstream coordinates from kind primitives at module-startup).
The `wsl` kind delivers a project-controlled WSL rootfs tar that replaces the Microsoft-Store-appx-mutable distribution today's caparison-w depends on;
the `podvm-*` kinds collapse `rbv_PodmanVM.sh`'s existing GHCR mirror into GAR under the unified verb.
_Trigger_: when Windows host workload requires a project-controlled WSL distro, when podman VM supply chain needs project-owned bytes in GAR, or when the `mirror`/`reliquary` machinery accumulates enough friction to motivate consolidation.
_Reference_: RBSLE-lode_ensconce.adoc (the bole-kind capture spec);
RBSRV-RegimeVessel.adoc (ORIGIN/ANCHOR precedent, kind-enum-gated variable groups pattern);
`Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/FUTURE/rbv_PodmanVM.sh` (existing podman-vm fan-out machinery).

+**Substrate Lode consumption — wsl/podvm host-side election and provisioning.**

+Lode capture is landed: per-kind capture verbs (`ensconce`/`conclave`/`underpin`/`immure` — `enshrine` retired) each emit one provenance-enveloped GAR package, the **Lode**, parallel to {rbtga_ark} on the built side and identified by its touchmark;
+intent stays distributed per kind — no unified recipe noun, no resolved-FQIN field — generalizing RBRV's ORIGIN/ANCHOR pattern.

+What remains is the consumption half for the substrate kinds: the workload's `wsl --import` consuming a verified rootfs Lode in place of the Microsoft-Store-appx-mutable distribution today's caparison-w depends on, and podman machine provisioning consuming a podvm Lode in place of quay's rapidly-churning upstream.
+Substrate touchmarks are Director-pinned at the nameplate and materialized at charge — the Retriever receives versions, never elects them.
+Open: which host-tier regime backs node-side election/provisioning (station regime vs BURN node profile vs a new host-config regime).
+_Trigger_: when the Windows host workload moves onto the project-controlled WSL distro, or when podman machine provisioning must survive quay retention.
+_Reference_: RBSLU-lode_underpin.adoc, RBSLI-lode_immure.adoc (the substrate capture specs);
+RBSDY-director_yoke.adoc (build-time election precedent);
+BUSJCW CaparisonWindows, BUSJGW GarrisonWsl (the `wsl --export` seed path this retires).
